Tafseer:
-1 Word Research and Explanation of Verses 
Qaf wal-qur'anil-majid -1
The Quran is itself a witness to its own truth.
Qaf is the name of this Surah. The subject (mubtada), as we have been clarifying in various places, is omitted here as well. If you unfold it, the meaning will be that this is Surah Qaf.
The meaning of 'majid' is glorious, exalted, and magnificent. This has been used in the Quran for Allah Almighty as well, and also as an attribute of the Quran. Every discourse is a manifestation of the attributes of its speaker. For this reason, just as Allah Almighty is Glorious and Exalted, similarly His Discourse is also glorious and exalted, and this excellence and glory is evident from each and every verse of the Quran. It is impossible for a person of taste to listen to or read the Quran and not be affected and overawed by its grandeur and power. If someone is not affected by its grandeur and majesty, he is either extremely dull-witted or his heart has become completely blackened. Let alone humans, if this Quran had been sent down upon the mountains, they too, as mentioned in the Quran, would have shattered to pieces out of the fear of Allah Almighty.
'Wal-qur'anil-majid' is an oath statement. Allah Almighty has sworn an oath by this magnificent and exalted Book, and regarding oaths, we have been clarifying in various places in this book that all such oaths in the Quran are by way of testimony. That is, the oath is sworn as an evidence for the object of the oath (muqsam 'alayh). This oath is also an evidence for the object of the oath, even though it is omitted. The object of the oath is omitted in those places where the context of the discourse is sufficient to clarify it. A highly clear example of this is present in Surah Sad. It is stated: 'Sad wal-qur'ani dhi-dh-dhikr balilladhina kafaru fi 'izzatin wa shiqaq' (1-2) (For its explanation, see Tadabbur-i-Quran, Volume 5, pages 509-510). By reflecting, it becomes known that here the oath by the grandeur and majesty of the Quran was sworn in refutation of those people who used to declare it as a kind of poetry, fortune-telling, sorcery, or satanic projection. Presenting the grandeur of the Quran as a testimony, they were warned that it is nothing of the sort. It is a witness by its very existence that its source is not these lowly things, but rather it is the revelation sent down by Allah Almighty, the medium of which is Gabriel the Trustworthy, and the source of which is the Preserved Tablet (Lawh-i-Mahfuz). Exactly with this kind of context, it is stated in Surah Al-Buruj:
'Bal huwa qur'anum majid, fi lawhim mahfuz' (21-22)
Nay, but it is a glorious Quran, and its source is in a Preserved Tablet.
This same matter was stated in Surah At-Takwir as follows:
'Innahu la-qawlu rasulin karim, dhi quwwatin 'inda dhi-l-'arshi makin, muta'in thamma amin. Wa ma sahibukum bi-majnun. Wa laqad ra'ahu bil-ufuqil-mubin. Wa ma huwa 'alal-ghaybi bi-danin. Wa ma huwa bi-qawli shaytanir-rajim. Fa-ayna tadhhabun. In huwa illa dhikrul-lil-'alamin' (19-27)
Indeed, it is the word of an honored messenger, possessing power and having an established position with the Owner of the Throne. He is obeyed there and, moreover, trustworthy. And your companion is not a madman. And he has already seen him on the clear horizon. And he is not a concealer of the unseen. And it is not the word of an accursed devil. So where are you going? It is not except a reminder to the worlds.
Toward this very aspect of the grandeur of the Quran, phrases like 'la ya'tihil-batilu mim bayni yadayhi wa la min khalfihi' (Fussilat: 42) (Falsehood cannot come to it from before it or from behind it) and 'la yamassuhu illal-mutahharun' (Al-Waqi'ah: 79) (And none touch it except the purified ones) and other verses of this theme are also pointing. At the end of Surah Ash-Shu'ara, the arguments described to acquit the Quran from the accusation of fortune-telling and poetry have already been explained in the commentary of that Surah. Especially take a look at what has been written under 'wa ma tanazzalat bihi-sh-shayatin, wa ma yambaghi lahum wa ma yastati'un' (Ash-Shu'ara: 210-211).
The meaning is that those people who are ignoring the warning of the Resurrection given by the Quran by making the excuse that this is not divine revelation but rather satanic projection, the miraculous eloquence of the Quran and its divine wisdom are sufficient for their refutation. Foolish are those who consider it the speech of some jinn or devil. This is not the speech of a jinn or devil, but the speech sent down by the Almighty, the All-Wise God.
'The essence of Jam-i-Jam is from the mine of another world.'

Bal 'ajibu an ja'ahum mundhirum minhum fa-qalal-kafiruna hadha shay'un 'ajib. A-idha mitna wa kunna turaba dhalika raj'um ba'id (2-3)
The Real Cause of the Opponents' Opposition 
Here, 'bal' (nay/but) is proof of the fact that the Quran has declared this objection merely as an excuse to flee from reality. If you unfold the implications of this 'bal', the whole matter will be like this: those who declare the Quran to be a projection of jinns and devils, the grandeur and majesty of the Quran is sufficient for their refutation. The real cause of their evasion is not what they are displaying, but rather it is that they are amazed that a person from among themselves has risen as a warner for them, who is warning them that after death they will be raised again. Since accepting a man from among themselves as the Messenger of God is highly burdensome upon their hearts, they started saying in its denial, 'How on earth is it possible that after dying and after the body rots and decomposes, people will be raised! This being raised is highly far-fetched from reason!' Reflect well on this verse, and this reality will come forward: according to the Quran, the real cause of this flight of theirs is their arrogance (istikbar). They are not ready to accept a person from among themselves as the Messenger; for this reason, they are also deniers of the Quran being a divine revelation and declare the Resurrection to be an unimaginable thing. The meaning is that if they were not afflicted with arrogance, they are not so dull-witted that they could not distinguish between the Discourse of Allah and the absurdities of fortune-tellers, and remain incapable of understanding the arguments for the affirmation of the Resurrection that the Quran is presenting before them.
The details of this arrogance have passed in the preceding Surahs: that these people, first of all, do not believe in the guidance of any messenger for their guidance, and even if they do believe to some degree, their assumption is that if Allah Almighty had to send a messenger, He would have sent an angel as a messenger or would have made one of the chiefs of Mecca and Ta'if a messenger. In the presence of these chiefs, how is it possible that He would make a messenger out of a person who is a completely poor man?
The answers to these objections have already been given in detail in the preceding Surahs, especially in the fourth group whose comprehensive axis is the affirmation of messengership and which begins with Al-Furqan. Here, according to the requirement of the group's theme, those doubts of theirs have been addressed which they used to raise against the possibility of being brought back to life after death, and which they had made—as we indicated above—an excuse for opposing the Quran and the Messenger.

Qad 'alimna ma tanqusu-l-ardu minhum wa 'indana kitabun hafiz 4
Answer to the Doubts of the Opponents 
It is stated that being brought back to life appears far-fetched from possibility to them because after the bodies rot, decompose, and mix with the dust, to gather their parts from the layers of the earth is, in their opinion, impossible. This misunderstanding occurred because they analogized the knowledge of Allah Almighty to their own knowledge, whereas the knowledge of Allah Almighty encompasses everything. He knows all those parts of the human body that the earth decomposes. No thing is hidden from Him. He who created everything and by whose command alone death overcomes everything, how can any thing remain hidden from Him! 'A-la ya'lamu man khalaq' (Al-Mulk: 14) (Does He not know who created?). And when He knows, whenever He wills, He will gather all those parts and reform the body of everyone anew. He will not face the slightest difficulty in this task. He who gave existence to everything from absolute non-existence and faced no difficulty in it, why would He face difficulty in re-gathering its scattered parts?
'Wa 'indana kitabun hafiz', meaning: apart from His personal knowledge, Allah Almighty has also established a ledger to keep the entire record of everything protected, in which it is also recorded which person is buried in which expanse of the earth, where the parts of his body are, and all the words and deeds of every person are also recorded in it. Just as the ignorant face this doubt regarding the Resurrection as to whose power it is to gather the parts of the human body after it rots, decomposes, and mixes with the soil, and to reform them in the shape of a body anew, similarly, this doubt also arises as to who can keep a protected record of every word and deed of every person so that one day He may sit to reckon everyone and give punishment and reward to everyone? This piece has cleared this doubt as well: that Allah Almighty has kept the record of all the words and actions of the people protected in a ledger.
This matter of keeping a protected record could have been a wonder for the Arabs of the period of ignorance, but in this era, after knowing the discoveries that science has made, if a person doubts this claim of the Quran, then no argument, no matter how great, can convince such stubborn ones. These people will believe only when their books of deeds are handed into their hands, but believing at that time will be completely useless.

Bal kadhhabu bil-haqqi lamma ja'ahum fa-hum fi amrim marij 5
The word 'haqq' (truth) has come for the Resurrection in Verse 19 and Verse 42 of this very Surah, but here the context of 'lamma ja'ahum' (when it came to them) indicates that it refers to the Quran, which was informing the people of the Resurrection but the people were denying it, and as an excuse for its denial, they were presenting those doubts against the Resurrection which were described above. It is stated that these people, through these doubts of theirs, are trying to give the impression as if the Resurrection is in reality a highly far-fetched thing from possibility, and that their denial of it is based on reasonable grounds that they possess. But the reality is that they have denied a completely manifest truth, and that too at the time when it came before them completely clearly.
From 'lamma ja'ahum', this matter emerges that the Resurrection of which the Holy Quran is giving news was a truth even before, but if until now these people remained trapped in doubts regarding it, they also had some excuse that they were unlettered people unfamiliar with the Quran and the Book. But now what excuse can they make when they are denying a truth that is shining over their heads like the midday sun!
The Contradiction of Thought of the Deniers of Resurrection 
'Fa-hum fi amrim marij': The experts of lexicography explain 'amr-i-marij' with the words 'amr-i-mukhtalit' (confused matter) or 'amr-i-multabis' (perplexed matter); meaning, a situation in which there is a highly clear kind of contradiction and conflict. The meaning of 'marj' is mixing, i.e., jumbling up. In Surah Ar-Rahman, it is stated: 'Maraja-l-bahrayni yaltaqiyan' (19) (He released the two seas, meeting side by side). By denying the Quran, the situation that the deniers faced is exactly characterized by this: that these people had become trapped in a manifest kind of contradiction of thought. On one side, they confess God and all those attributes of His that make the Resurrection necessary; on the other side, they deny the Resurrection, which is the completely manifest requirement of this confession. In this manner, they have become stuck in such a mental entanglement from which no way out is appearing to them. The single way of salvation from this entanglement is the very one that the Quran is telling them, but they are not ready to accept it, even though the inevitable result of denying the truth is that all the arguments a man invents to prove his denial justifiable lay bare the hollowness of his stance. The Quran has pointed toward this very contradiction of thought of theirs in Surah Adh-Dhariyat, which is its sister Surah, with the words: 'Innakum la-fi qawlim mukhtalif' (8) (Indeed, you are in a severe kind of contradiction). The full explanation of this issue has already passed under Verse 66 of Surah An-Naml: 'Bal-iddaraka 'ilmuhum fi-l-akhirati...' If details are required, please take a look at it.
It should be kept in mind here that in the misguidance of man, the greatest role is played by this very contradiction of thought of his: either due to his easy-going nature he gathers all kinds of raw and dry theories in his mind, or in following his self-desire, along with correct theories and beliefs, he also tries to gather false theories, the result of which emerges that his life becomes a collection of opposites. If man regularly evaluates his thoughts, does not let the ability of critique die, and does not try to join falsehood with the truth in following the desires of the self, he can stay protected from this mischief of Satan, but such people are few.

A-fa-lam yanzuru ila-s-sama'i fawqahum kayfa banaynaha wa zayyannaha wa ma laha min furuj. Wal-arda madadnaha wa alqayna fiha rawasiya wa ambatna fiha min kulli zawjim bahij. Tabsirataw-wa dhikra li-kulli 'abdim munib (6-8)
Pointing Toward the Signs of Allah Almighty 
Here, Allah Almighty has drawn the attention of the deniers of the Resurrection toward those manifest signs of His power, providence (Rububiyyah), and wisdom that are seen everywhere above and below, and are sufficient to produce insight and reminder inside every person in whose chest is a heart that can be affected and can turn.
First of all, He drew attention toward His magnificent power and wisdom: have they never raised a glance toward the sky above them to see how We raised it, decorated it with stars, and it is the miracle of Our power and wisdom that they cannot point out any breach anywhere in this pillarless roof. The meaning is that He whose miracle of power and wisdom they see over their heads, will it become difficult for Him to create them again after their death?
After that, along with power and wisdom, He also drew attention toward the arrangement of His providence and nourishment. He stated that they should look below them at how We have spread the earth beneath their feet, and to maintain its balance, We have driven the pegs of mountains inside it, and We have grown all kinds of things in it that serve as their food, and whose beautiful sight also delights their vision. The meaning is that the Provider whose power, wisdom, and provider-nature they are witnessing, is it difficult for Him to raise them up alive again after they die? Will the Provider who has made this arrangement for their nourishment leave them abandoned in this manner that they eat, drink, enjoy luxury, and no questioning will ever take place with them in this chapter?
This world is a school of realization in its existence.
'Tabsirataw-wa dhikra li-kulli 'abdim munib', meaning: Allah Almighty has made these glories of His power, wisdom, and providence manifest inside the heavens and the earth so that those people who are attentive may produce this insight and reminder inside themselves. By 'tabsirah' is meant producing insight inside the eyes so that they can pass beyond the outward and reach that reality toward which the outward is guiding. And by 'dhikra' is meant removing the veil of heedlessness. The meaning is that Allah Almighty has filled every nook and corner of the sky and earth with such wonders and miracles that are sufficient to lift the veils of the eyes and to shake and awaken the hearts. But these miracles are effective upon those inside whom the sense of receptivity is present. Those who, due to their worship of the perceptible, have let this subtle sense of theirs die, for them this entire universe is a world of darkness. Remember here that matter toward which we have pointed in various places in this book: that this world was not dependent for its survival upon all these colorful varieties and flower-craftings that are manifest in its every corner, but nature has shown its glories inside it with this generosity for the very reason that man's subtle sense, which is affected and awakened by power, wisdom, beauty, and grace and generosity, should awaken, and he should learn and understand the lessons of wisdom that are inscribed on each and every leaf of this garden. But for this, it is necessary that the ability to attend and turn (inabat) which the Creator has deposited inside every human being, he should bring it into use. If a person does not use this ability of his, Allah Almighty does not care for such dull and insensitive animals. He has made man a purposeful entity, the inevitable demand of which is that he should value the consciousness granted to him and take steps ahead under its guidance. If he does so, he is granted more light from God; otherwise, whatever is granted to him is also seized in retribution for his lack of appreciation.
The subject described in these verses has been described in other places of the Quran as well. For the satisfaction of the heart and openness of the chest, we copy some references here. In Surah Bani Isra'il, addressing those who considered being brought back to life after death as far-fetched, it was stated:
'A-wa-lam yaraw annallahalladhi khalaqa-s-samawati wal-arda qadirun 'ala ay-yakhluqa mithlahum' (Bani Isra'il: 99)
Have they not considered that Allah, who created the heavens and the earth, is Able to create the like of them?
The answer to this same doubt of the deniers of the Resurrection was given in Surah An-Nazi'at in these words:
'A-antum ashaddu khalqan am-is-sama' banaha. Rafa'a samkaha fa-sawwaha. Wa aghtasha laylaha wa akhraja duhaha. Wal-arda ba'da dhalika dahaha. Akhraja minha ma'aha wa mar'aha. Wal-jibala arsaha. Mata'al-lakum wa li-an'amikum' (An-Nazi'at: 27-33)
Are you a more difficult creation or is the heaven? He constructed it. He raised its ceiling and proportioned it. And He darkened its night and extracted its brightness. And the earth after that He spread. He extracted from it its water and its pasture. And the mountains He set firmly. As provision for you and your cattle.
Reflect on these verses, and it will be known that within them, all those aspects of power, grandeur, wisdom, and providence have condensed with some more expanse, from which arguments have been drawn in the under-discussion verses of Surah Qaf for the occurrence of the Resurrection, its necessity, and its being a requirement of the divine attributes.

Wa nazzalna mina-s-sama'i ma'am-mubarakan fa-ambatna bihi jannatiw-wa habbal-hasid. Wan-nakhla basigatil-laha tal'un-nadid. Rizqal-lil-'ibadi wa ahyayna bihi baldatam-mayta kadhali-l-khuruj (9-11) 
Pointing Toward the Signs of Heaven and Earth from Another Aspect 
This drew attention toward the signs of heaven and earth from another angle, from which testimony for Monotheism (Tawhid) is also gained from the aspect of harmony between heaven and earth. From the causes of providence deposited inside them, the necessity of reward and punishment also comes forward, and from the fresh life that appears inside the dead earth due to rain, the occurrence of life after death is also witnessed by every person. By 'ma'am-mubarakan' (blessed water) is meant that rain which is a cause of fertility and freshness. Sometimes such rain also occurs which becomes a cause of destruction instead of fertility, and it takes the form of a punishment for nations. Here, the attribute of 'mubarak' is for the removal of this very doubt.
By 'habbal-hasid' (grain of harvest) are meant those commodities that are reaped and stored. For example, wheat and barley, etc. Along with gardens, the purpose of mentioning 'habbal-hasid' is to draw attention toward that arrangement of providence which the Lord of this universe has made for His servants: that He created fresh and delicious fruits for the people in the gardens and also grew food grains in the fields, which are cut upon ripening, stored, and come to regular use.
'Wan-nakhla basigatil-laha tal'un-nadid': Apparently, after mentioning gardens (jannat), there was no need to mention date-palms (nakhl), but this mention of the specific after the general occurred because this was the specific fruit of the Arab, which was the best fruit for them and to a large extent fulfilled their nutritional need too. Pointing toward its tall stature and its layered clusters is to produce the sense of observing the universe and the spirit of gratitude inside the addressee, so that he sees these signs of power and takes from them that effect which a sensitive and awake heart ought to take.
'Rizqal-lil-'ibad', meaning: the layered clusters are testifying by their very form that the Creator has spent His power, wisdom, and craftsmanship upon them with this generosity for the very reason that His servants may benefit from them, observe the glories of His wisdom and providence inside them, and recognize the right of these blessings, that one day questioning must inevitably take place regarding them from Him.
'Wa ahyayna bihi baldatam-maytan kadhalika-l-khuruj': This is that original objective which holds the status of the axis of this Surah. It is stated that the rain whose blessings you witness, it is also the miracle of this same rain that the earth which becomes completely dead, waterless, and vegetationless, having no sign of any green or growth in any corner of it, upon the falling of a drop of rain, a fresh life appears from every inch of it. Apply analogy from this to being raised alive again after death as well, of which you are being informed but you are considering it impossible.

Kadhhabat qablahum qawmu nuhin-wa ashabur-rassi wa thamud. Wa 'adun-wa fir'awnu wa ikhwanu lut. Wa ashabul-aykati wa qawmu tubba'in kullun kadhhabar-rusula fa-haqqa wa'id (12-14)
Threat to the Quraysh 
This is a threat to the Quraysh: that before them, the people of Noah, the companions of the Rass, Thamud, 'Ad, Pharaoh, the people of Lot, the companions of the thicket (Aykah), and the people of Tubba' also denied in this manner when Allah clarified the truth to them completely through His messengers. Ultimately, the end of this denial of which they were informed manifested before them. Similarly, these people too, if they remain stubborn upon this denial, the punishment with which they are being threatened will inevitably come, and no power of heaven and earth will be able to save them from the grasp of God. This points to that divine Sunnah which manifests inevitably after the completion of proof through a messenger. We have been regularly clarifying it.
Pointing toward this threat has occurred above in Verse 5 as well, but after that hint there, the direction of the discourse turned toward the description of the arguments for the Return. After the arguments, He explained this hint clearly: that while they are denying with this daring, they should keep before them the end of their predecessor nations who reached the retribution for their conduct as a result of denying the messengers.
Ashab ar-Rass 
All the nations whose mention has occurred here, references to them from different aspects have passed in the preceding Surahs as well, and we have explained their historical status according to the need. The mention of Ashab ar-Rass occurred in Verse 38 of Surah Al-Furqan. Under it, we have given references to the statements of commentators about them and have also expressed our opinion. Although the order in the mention of the nations here is not historical, the mention of Ashab ar-Rass immediately after the people of Noah is certainly a context clue that they belong to the ancient destroyed nations, the history of most of whom has become completely non-existent.
From the words 'kullun kadhhabar-rusul', this matter also emerges that a messenger was sent toward them whom they denied, and as a result of that, the punishment of Allah Almighty came upon them which destroyed them. In the books of commentary, a tradition has also been copied that this nation had buried its messenger in a well. 'Rass' means a well, for which reason their name became Ashab ar-Rass. But this matter does not seem correct. The killing of any of the messengers at the hands of his nation is not proven. Even if Rass means a well, the correctness of this event is not necessary for attribution to it.
The cause for not mentioning his nation along with Pharaoh is that the status of the original criminal was possessed, in reality, by Pharaoh alone. He misguided his nation. In Surah Taha, it is stated: 'wa adalla fir'awnu qawmahu wa ma hada' (79) (And Pharaoh misguided his nation and did not guide them right). This same matter was stated in other words as follows: 'idhhab ila fir'awna innahu tagha' (24) (Go to Pharaoh, indeed he has become highly rebellious).

The mention of the people of Tubba' has passed in the commentary of Surah Ad-Dukhan. A-fa-'ayiyna bil-khalqil-awwal bal hum fi labsim-min khalqin jadid 15
The Artist Draws the Second Painting Better Than the First 
In the verses 6-11 above, you saw that Allah Almighty, with special arrangement, drew attention toward that power and wisdom of His which is manifest from every corner of heaven and earth, and the purpose of it—as we indicated above—was to convince those people who imagined being raised up alive anew after death as far-fetched from possibility. He has placed this question before those very people: let these people look at Our heavens and Our earth and tell, did We fail in making them the first time? The explanation of the meaning of 'a'yiya bil-amr' has been done by the experts of lexicography with the words 'idha lam yahtadi li-wajhi 'amalihi'; meaning, a person becomes incapable of performing a task. It does not enter his understanding as to how it should be executed.
'Bal hum fi labsim-min khalqin jadid', meaning: these people cannot dare to say that We were incapable of creating the heavens and the earth the first time. They confess that the Creator of heaven and earth is We alone. However, they possess hesitation in this chapter that We will not be able to create them anew! In my view, the style of this sentence is sarcastic; the meaning is that when they are seeing with open eyes that no obstacle faced Us in creating the first time, why will an obstacle face Us a second time! Someone should ask these smart alecks, is it difficult to make a thing the first time or the second time? If an artist can draw a second painting better in comparison to the first painting, then why can We not create the world anew with more ease?

2 The Subject of the Upcoming Verses 16-35 
In the upcoming verses, first the arrangement has been mentioned which Allah Almighty has made to keep the record of every person's words and actions. After that, a picture has been drawn of the manifestation of the Resurrection and the presentation of the criminals. After that is the description of throwing the criminals into Hell and their mutual recrimination. Then there is the detail of that grace and reward which Allah Almighty will bestow upon His righteous servants. Please recite the verses.
-3 Word Research and Explanation of Verses 
Wa laqad khalaqna-l-insana wa na'lamu ma tuwaswisu bihi nafsuh wa nahnu aqrabu ilayhi min habli-l-warid 16
The Knowledge of Allah Almighty Encompasses Everything 
Where the ignorant are afflicted with this doubt regarding the Resurrection as to whose power it is to gather all the parts of the body and stand them up alive anew after man dies and turns to dust in the earth, similarly, many are also afflicted with this doubt as to who can keep a record of all the words and actions of each and every person in privacy and public so that one day He may sit to reckon everyone and give reward and punishment to everyone. Both these doubts are completely fundamental. For this reason, in Verse 4 above, the answer to both has been given in a summary manner. Then, with detail, the refutation of the first doubt was carried out. Now this addresses the second doubt and answers it with arguments. It is stated that We have created man, and let alone his words and actions, even the whispers that cross his heart, We know them. The meaning is that since Allah Almighty is the Creator—He alone created man and proportioned his every joint and limb—it is necessary that He should be well-aware of the setting and function of all his parts. Toward this very reality, attention has been drawn in other words as follows: 'A-la ya'lamu man khalaq' (Al-Mulk: 14) (Does He not know who created?). If the Creator is not aware of all the details of His creation, how will He arrange its protection and its survival?
'Wa nahnu aqrabu ilayhi min habli-l-warid': 'Warid' means the life-vein (jugular vein). This is a well-known idiom of the Arabic language that comes to express the extreme degree of closeness. It is stated that no person should remain in this misunderstanding that if he is not seeing Us, We are far from him. We are closer to every person than even his jugular vein. Our knowledge and Our power encompass every person from every aspect, and his entire outward and inward is before Our eyes at every moment.

Idh yatalaqqa-l-mutalaqqiyani 'ani-l-yamini wa 'ani-sh-shimali qa'id. Ma yalfizu min qawlin illa ladayhi raqibun 'atid (17-18)
Further Arrangement to Keep the Record of People's Actions and Words 
In 'ani-l-yamini wa 'ani-sh-shimali qa'id', the structure of the discourse is like this: 'ani-l-yamini qa'idun wa 'ani-sh-shimali qa'id' (one sitting on the right, another sitting on the left). According to the well-known rule of Arabic, by the demand of conciseness, the word 'qa'id' is omitted in one place. Examples of this have passed behind.
This is the description of that further arrangement which Allah Almighty has made to keep the record of people's words and actions protected. First of all, Allah Almighty is Himself well-aware of even the whispers and thoughts of the heart of every person; furthermore, for the completion of proof, He has also made this arrangement that He has appointed two angels over every person, one of whom sits on his right, another on the left. The moment he utters any word, he finds an alert watcher present with him to note it down.
Although the mention of actions being noted down is not in the words here, they will be considered included in it by way of dominance (ala sabil al-taghlib), because when this arrangement is there for the noting down of these words issued from the tongue, then the arrangement for the noting down of actions performed by the hands and feet must be present to a greater degree. From the Ahadith, it is known that there is a division of work between these two angels. The angel appointed on the right side keeps the record of righteous actions and words. The one on the left side keeps the record of bad actions and words. In the matter of testimony, since the testimony of two witnesses is considered credible in the religion, for this reason, Allah Almighty also appointed two angels.
From the verse, this matter also emerges that the whispers and thoughts of the heart of man are outside the reach of these angels. The Unseen is known only by Allah Almighty, and this is His covering of secrets that He kept the knowledge of the secrets of the hearts of the servants limited to Himself alone.
Another Arrangement for the Completion of Proof 
Apart from this arrangement, another arrangement for the completion of proof has also been made by Allah Almighty. That is that the hands, feet, ears, and eyes of man will also give testimony before Allah Almighty of all his words and actions. Whatever we speak from the tongue, its entire record our ears are protecting, and whatever we see, its entire movie is in the custody of our eyes. This same is the case of our other limbs and organs. Whatever we say or do, we do all before the guards appointed by God and through the limbs and organs granted by Him. Then how great a foolishness it is to imagine how Allah Almighty can know each and every word and deed of ours to sit to reckon them!
In 'idh yatalaqqa-l-mutalaqqiyan', according to the well-known rule of Arabic, a verb is omitted before 'idh'. Since here the heedless are being reminded of a fundamental reality, some such verb will be considered omitted through which the addressee is warned. Meaning: keep in mind, take care. I have observed this in the translation. The author of Al-Kashshaf has adopted another form, but I do not agree with it.

The Resurrection Is a Bound-to-Happen Event 
Wa ja'at sakratu-l-mawti bil-haqq dhalika ma kunta minhu tahid 19
By 'haqq' (truth) is meant that very Resurrection of which the Quran was giving news and the denial of which was mentioned above in Verse 5. Since it is a bound-to-happen and an unalterable reality, for this reason, it has been expressed by 'haqq'. The past tense is to express its certainty. The meaning is that do not imagine it to be very far, but consider it an arrived thing. Whatever days of life are remaining, only that much is the delay in the arrival of the Resurrection. The moment the unconsciousness of death overcomes, the Resurrection is, as it were, standing right next to it. At that time, the situation will itself tell you that the thing from which you used to evade has finally arrived.
The Resurrection Is Attached to the Death of Every Person 
This same reality of the Resurrection has been described in the Ahadith as well. In a Hadith, it is stated: 'man mata faqad qamat qiyamatuhu' (whoever dies, his Resurrection has arrived). The cause of this is that the life of the barrier-world (Barzakh) after death is, in reality, the preface to the Resurrection itself. Along with death, the beginning of the states of the world of the Hereafter takes place. The people of faith get the testimony of the truth of faith and its good end, and before the disbelievers, the beginning of the manifestation of that end takes place which they used to deny in the life of the world. After this, room for any doubt does not remain for anyone either.
The Correct Characterization of the Mind of Its Deniers Regarding the Resurrection 
'Dhalika ma kunta minhu tahid': This matter can be the characterization of the state, and can be by way of speech too. The meaning is that at that time you will see that the thing from which you used to evade has finally arrived over the head. The exact meaning of 'hada yahidu' is to evade from a path and walk. This is the exact characterization of the attitude of those people regarding the Resurrection who follow the desires of their self. They spend life being careless of the Resurrection, so it is not because the arguments for the Resurrection are hidden from them—its signs are present at every step—but those who do not want to adopt the straight path evade them and walk. But what will they do at that time when it stands right before them?

Wa nufikha fi-s-suri dhalika yawmul-wa'id 20
Meaning: after this, it is merely the stage of the blowing of the Trumpet. The Trumpet will be blown and that day will manifest with which you are being threatened and you are denying it. This too, to express its certainty, has been uttered with the past tense so that it becomes pictured before the eyes. The meaning is that do not remain in this misunderstanding that life, then death, then Barzakh, then gathering and resurrection is a matter of a very far time. When the Trumpet is blown, you will feel that the period you considered very long passed in the blinking of an eye.

Wa ja'at kullu nafsim-ma'aha sa'iquw-wa shahid (21)
This is the stage after the blowing of the Trumpet. On that day, every soul will be brought for presentation before its Lord in such a manner that one angel will be appointed behind it to drive it, and another with the record of its words and actions for testimony. This very meaning is apparent from the verse, and this same is transmitted from the predecessors. Some people have taken both 'sa'iq' (driver) and 'shahid' (witness) to mean a single angel, implying that he will bring it by driving and he will also give testimony. Some people have taken 'sa'iq' to mean the angel, but 'shahid' to mean the man and his action. Both these statements are weak in our view. The first is weak from the aspect of Arabic, and in the second there is an obvious affectation which the words of the verse are refusing.
Some people have taken them to mean those very two angels whose mention passed above in Verse 17, but this matter also appears weak. These two angels, it is clear from the words of the verse, are appointed to write the words and actions of the man. If they themselves have to present the man to bring him to the court of God, both should have a single status. That is, both should present themselves as witnesses. No cause enters the understanding as to why, when both appointees had a single status, the status of one would change? In any case, none of these statements is free from weakness. The matter that emerges from the words of the verse is the very one to which we referred above: that after the blowing of the Trumpet, the presentation of every person in the court of Allah Almighty is to be in such a manner that one angel will be behind him to drive him, and another ahead of him with the record of all words and actions for testimony.

Laqad kunta fi ghaflatim-min hadha fa-kashafna 'anka ghita'aka fa-basaruka-l-yawma hadid (22)
An Admonition at the Time of Presentation 
At that time, Allah Almighty will warn him: you remained in heedlessness of this day, so today We have removed your veil from before your eyes.
'Fa-basaruka-l-yawma hadid': This phrase will be by way of sarcasm for his exposure, implying: today your sight is very sharp! The thing whose possibility you did not see anywhere far and wide, how has its every corner become unveiled before you today?
This verse does not contradict that verse in which it has been stated that the deniers of the signs of Allah will be raised blind on the Day of Resurrection. The context and placement of both we have clarified.

Wa qala qarinuhu hadha ma ladayya 'atid 23
Presentation of the Criminal in the Court 
The context is proof that by 'qarin' (companion) is meant one of those very two who will present themselves with him in the divine court in the capacity of 'sa'iq' and 'shahid'. Some people have taken it to mean that devil who is imposed upon the one who turns away from Allah Almighty, but this matter does not seem correct. This devil will neither be in a position to be able to say regarding anyone that 'this person who was in my custody is present,' nor will he take the responsibility of misguiding anyone on that day. Ahead, his statement is coming that when the people trapped in his net will want to throw the responsibility of their misguiding upon him, he will make a clear declaration of disavowal: 'Rabbana ma atghaytuhu wa lakin kana fi dalalim ba'id' (Qaf: 27) (Our Lord, I did not make him rebellious, but he was himself in a far-gone misguidance). For this reason, in our view, it means one of those very two. As for the question of which of them is meant, it is difficult to give a decisive answer. It can mean the 'shahid' and it can mean the 'sa'iq' as well, because this statement can be made by both: 'this who was in my custody is present.' Consequently, the masters of interpretation have differed in it too. My inclination is toward this that this statement will be made by the 'sa'iq'. The cause of this is that the criminal will be originally in his charge, and upon him alone will be this responsibility to present him in the court. Consequently, when he presents him in the court, like a duty-conscious soldier, he will express his acquittal from his duty in these words: 'hadha ma ladayya 'atid' (this who is in my custody is present before the presence!).

Alqiya fi jahannama kulla kaffarin 'anid 24
A Question and Its Answer 
'Alqiya' is a dual form, for which reason the question arises as to which two angels this command will be given. Generally, the masters of interpretation have declared its addressees to be those very two angels whose mention has passed above with the words 'sa'iq' and 'shahid'. To them will be the command: throw every ungrateful معاند (obstinate opponent) into Hell. No weakness appears apparently in this interpretation. Giving this command to these two does not make it necessary that they themselves throw him into Hell too. This is merely the statement of the command; in its execution, they will hand over the criminal to the angels who throw into Hell, and they will throw him into that layer of Hell which will be specific for criminals of this degree.
Regarding this dual form, Zamakhshari has also copied a statement of the famous scholar of language, Mubarrad: that the eloquent of the Arabs sometimes use the dual form merely for the meaning of the repetition of the verb. For example, the opening verse of the poem of Imru' al-Qays is: 'Qifa nabki min dhikra habibiw-wa manzili'. People generally translate it like this: 'O my two companions, stop a while, let us shed two tears in the memory of the beloved and the abode of the beloved.' In the light of this statement of Mubarrad, the meaning of 'qifa' will be only 'stop, stop'. There is no need to assume two addressees. Similarly, the meaning of 'alqiya' in the verse according to him will be 'throw, throw'. The objective from it is the expression of wrath and the emphasis of the command, it does not argue whether this command will be given to two angels or to more than two angels. It should be clear here that this same matter was stated in Surah Al-Haqqah with the plural form: 'Khudhuhu fa-ghulluhu thumma-l-jahima salluhu thumma fi silsilatin dhar'uha sab'una dhira'an faslukuh' (30-32) (Seize him and shackle him, then into Hellfire drive him, then into a chain whose length is seventy cubits insert him). I have given the reference to this statement merely for the purpose of reflection and research. I do not possess full certainty upon it. However, if from further research satisfactory evidences are arranged in its favor, it will help in solving some difficulties. As far as the states of the Hereafter are concerned, the path of safety regarding them in my view is that whatever meaning is apparent from the words of the Quran, man should believe in it. He should not enter into too much searching and digging.
'Kulla kaffarin 'anid': The meaning of 'kaffar' is highly ungrateful and denier of the truth, and of 'anid' is obstinate opponent. The word 'kufr' originally comes for ungratefulness to God and the denial of His rights. In the rights of God, for a person of sound nature, there is no room for any argument and conflict; for this reason, their denier is not just a disbeliever (kafir) but a 'kaffar', and a further addition takes place to this 'kaffari' of his when he stands up becoming an obstinate opponent and adversary of those rights so that others also do not become their fulfillers.

Manna'il-lil-khayri mu'tadim-murib. Alladhi ja'ala ma'allahi ilahan akhara fa-alqiyahu fi-l-'adhabish-shadid (25-26)
Deniers of the Rights of God Will Inevitably Be Deniers of the Rights of the Servants Too 
Those who are deniers and obstinate opponents of the rights of God will inevitably be highly miserly, stingy, and preventers of others regarding the rights of His servants too, and along with that, they will be 'mu'tadi', i.e., transgressors of boundaries, usurpers of rights. The word 'khayr' (good), though general for all virtues and goodnesses, is well-known for the virtue of spending (infaq), which relates to the servants. Within the word 'manna'' (preventer), the meaning of both withholding and preventing is present. Those who are miserly do not content themselves merely with becoming miserly themselves, but try to make others miserly too so that the secret of their miserliness is not exposed. Furthermore, people of this sort are also 'mu'tadi'; they do not remain patient with this much that they sit suppressing those rights of others that are due upon them, but by committing transgression, they also want that whatever others possess should not remain with them either, but they themselves should become its owners. The meaning of 'murib' is one trapped in doubt. Though this word is general—whichever chapter, Monotheism or the Hereafter, doubt is in, it is included in its meaning—the well-known use of it in the Quran is for that thing which is regarding the Resurrection, which is the subject under discussion in this Surah. Here, the mention of this attribute has occurred as the source of all those attributes that were described above. Doubt regarding the Resurrection is the very thing that makes man ungrateful, obstinate, miserly, and a transgressor. Consequently, after the mention of all these diseases, He also pointed out that original disease from which all these diseases are produced.
'Alladhi ja'ala ma'allahi ilahan akhara': This in the end, changing the style, with special arrangement, mentioned polytheism (shirk). Polytheism is the destroyer of all religion and morals and the root of all corruption in the earth. Along with polytheism, even if someone believes in the Resurrection, this believing remains completely without effect. If a man is trapped in the illusion that a certain person is a partner of God or such a darling of His that he can make God agree to whatever matter he wants, then for him, the occurrence or non-occurrence of the Resurrection both became equal. In his assumption, keeping him pleased, he can do whatever he wants; who can become the catcher of his hand!
Here, let there be an eye also on that style of Arabic of which we have been making mention in various places in this book: that when the mention of attributes occurs without a particle of conjunction, its meaning is that all these attributes are present in the described entity at one and the same time. Along with that, let this point also be in view that here in the description of attributes, the order is from branches to principles. It began with ungratefulness and miserliness, and ended with the denial of the Resurrection and polytheism. From this, this reality became clear that if man is doubtful regarding the Resurrection and trapped in polytheism, his character becomes what was described in these verses.
'Fa-alqiyahu fi-l-'adhabish-shadid': This is the emphasis of that very 'alqiya' above. Above He stated: throw him into Hell. Then, after referring to his attributes, with more intensity and emphasis He stated: throw him into the severe punishment of Hell. Regarding Hell (Jahannam), this clarification has passed in its place that it will have seven gates and numerous layers. For the people of the mentioned attributes and character, there will be the command to insert them into its severe punishment.

Qala qarinuhu rabbana ma atghaytuhu wa lakin kana fi dalalim ba'id. Qala la takhtasimu ladayya wa qad qaddamtu ilaykum bil-wa'id (27-28)
This is describing the event after entering the enclosure of Hell: that after entering it, everyone will want to foist the blame of his misguiding upon that devil who, according to the divine Sunnah, is imposed upon those people who remain heedless of the remembrance of Allah. When the devil sees that an effort is being made to make him the criminal, he will step forward and present his defense: 'Rabbana ma atghaytuhu wa lakin kana fi dalalim ba'id' (O our Lord, I did not make him trapped in rebellion, but he was himself gone so far into misguidance that no possibility of return remained for him from there). This statement of the devil 'ma atghaytuhu' does not mean that he will deny giving the invitation to misguidance. There is specification in the Quran that he will confess that he gave the invitation necessarily, but the accepter of this invitation was this person himself, he did not compel him upon it. In another place, its explanation occurred as follows:
'Wa qala-sh-shaytanu lamma qudiya-l-amru innallaha wa'adakum wa'da-l-haqqi wa wa'adtukum fa-akhlaftukum wa ma kana liya 'alaykum min sultanin illa an da'awtukum fastajabtum li fa-la talumuni wa lumu anfusakum ma ana bi-musrikhikum wa ma antum bi-musrikhiyya' (Ibrahim: 22)
And when the matter has been decided, Satan will say, 'Indeed, Allah had promised you the promise of truth. And I promised you, but I betrayed you. And I had no authority over you except that I invited you, and you responded to me. So do not blame me; but blame yourselves. I cannot be your caller-for-help, nor can you be my caller-for-help.'
Within 'wa lakin kana fi dalalim ba'id', there is a pointer toward this reality that he had gone so far into misguidance that no possibility at all of return toward the truth had remained for him. This matter should be kept in mind here that those who distance themselves a lot from the path of goodness ultimately become deprived of the tawfiq for guidance and fall into the hands of the devil. It is as if the devil has presented the argument for this matter: that if this person fell into his hands, it was because due to his love for misguidance, he had become deserving of it according to the divine Sunnah.
It is also worth remembering here that just as the devil will express disavowal from his followers on the Day of Resurrection, similarly, those leaders will also refuse to take the responsibility for the misguiding of those people who were misguided in following them. This matter has been described in the Quran repeatedly. We present an example from Surah Ibrahim:
'Wa barazu lillahi jami'an fa-qala-d-du'afa'u lilladhina-stakbaru inna kunna lakum taba'an fa-hal antum mughnuna 'anna min 'adhabillahi min shay' qalu law hadanallahu la-hadaynakum sawa'un 'alayna a-jazi'na am sabarna ma lana mim mahis' (Ibrahim: 21)
And they will come out before Allah all together, and the weak will say to those who were arrogant, 'Indeed, we were your followers, so can you avail us anything against the punishment of Allah?' They will say, 'If Allah had guided us, we would have guided you. It is all the same for us whether we show intolerance or are patient, there is for us no place of escape.'
'Qala la takhtasimu ladayya wa qad qaddamtu ilaykum bil-wa'id': Upon this bickering, Allah Almighty will warn them: now before Me, this dispute of yours is completely useless. This blame-game cannot make anyone declared free from the responsibility of his misguiding. I had already informed everyone beforehand of My threat, for which reason the proof has been completed and room for any excuse has not remained for anyone. By the threat, the pointer is toward that threat of which Allah Almighty had informed at the exact time when Iblis challenged Allah Almighty: 'If You give me respite, I will misguide the majority of the children of Adam.' In its answer, Allah Almighty stated: 'Go, whoever you can misguide, misguide him; I also inform that whoever follows you, whether they be jinns or humans, I will fill Hell with all of you together.'
After this threat, Allah Almighty regularly sent His prophets and messengers who, coming from interval to interval, kept informing the people of the guidance of Allah Almighty as well as of the end of following the devil and his progeny. Those who became trapped in misguidance, it is obvious that no excuse remained for them. After this, the results they will face, they have, as it were, knowingly jumped into them themselves.

Ma yubaddalu-l-qawlu ladayya wa ma ana bi-zallamil-lil-'abid 29
This is a further explanation and emphasis of that same matter. He stated: the matter of which I have already informed beforehand, now by your foisting blame on one another or weeping and wailing, it cannot be changed. The promises of Allah and His threats are completely absolute and unalterable. Whatever is coming before you is the fruit of your own actions, for which reason suffer it now. I am not one to commit wrongdoing to My servants at all.
Explanation of a Style of Arabic 
In 'wa ma ana bi-zallamil-lil-'abid', the style of Arabic that is present, its explanation in more than one place we have carried out: that when negation comes upon hyperbole (mubalaghah), the objective from it is hyperbole in negation (mubalaghah fi-n-nafi). For this reason, its meaning will be that I am not one to commit wrongdoing to the servants at all. Our commentators and translators, since they are generally unaware of this style, take it in the meaning of 'laysa bi-zalim' (is not a wrongdoer), and translators follow them in their translations, whereas these words are an explicit carelessness from the Quran. It is a matter of reflection that if the objective was merely to say that Allah Almighty is not a wrongdoer to His servants, why would the simple style of the language be abandoned and the style of 'laysa bi-zallam' be adopted to express this meaning? In the Quran, this word and this style possess a specific place, without understanding which well, the correct interpretation of the verse is not possible.
Examples of the style are present in the speech of the Arabs. Among the poets of the period of ignorance, Imru' al-Qays used the structures of 'al-mar'u laysa bi-qattal' and 'al-mar'u laysa bi-fa''al' in his verses. His rival had threatened him with war and killing, so mocking him he said that 'he threatens me with killing, whereas in this coward there is not the slightest motive for killing and fighting.' 'Laysa bi-fa''al': there is no courage inside him to be able to do anything. Some other poets also, I remember, have used structures of this type, but unfortunately, at the time of writing, those diwans are not with me. However, whatever I have written, I possess full satisfaction upon its correctness.
In the Quran, this same matter has been stated in another style too. For example, in Surah An-Nisa, it is:
'Innallaha la yazlimu mithqala dharratin' (40)
Indeed, Allah does not wrong even the weight of an atom.
In Surah Yunus, it is:
'Innallaha la yazlimu-n-nasa shay'an' (44)
Indeed, Allah does not wrong the people at all.
If any person has this doubt that the rule of hyperbole in negation which we have described is not a universal rule, it does not cause any difference. For the correctness of our interpretation, this matter is sufficient that this rule exists in Arabic, even if it is only in the capacity of a partial part. When this rule exists, in the interpretation of the Quran, among all the masters of the art, this principle is accepted: that if in any verse the possibility of two interpretations exists, that interpretation will be given preference which is the best and apparently in accordance with the Quran. Here, several matters are present in favor of our interpretation:
...This is in accordance with the style of Arabic.
...This is befitting of the attributes of Allah Almighty, because He is, in any case, pure from every taint of wrongdoing. He will neither commit wrongdoing upon the good nor upon the bad.
...Support is gained for it from the parallels and evidences of the Quran.
Among our commentators, only the author of Al-Kashshaf has addressed the problem of this verse, but the manner in which he has tried to solve it, support for his Mu'tazilite school comes out from it necessarily, but along with that, some other questions arise which he did not address.
According to him, the meaning of the verse is that Allah Almighty, if He were to wrong His obedient servants, it would become a great wrongdoing, and Allah Almighty is not a very great wrongdoer.
Upon this interpretation of the author of Al-Kashshaf, though many objections are raised, we, for the sake of conciseness, will mention only two matters:
One is that in the verse, there is no specification of the obedient or the disobedient; rather, the word is 'abid' (servants), which is general to all servants, whether they be good or bad. Relating to everyone, this matter has been stated that Allah Almighty is not one to commit wrongdoing upon them. For this reason, this allocation and specification is completely without evidence, rather contrary to the words of the Quran.
Second is that cast a glance at the context of the verse, and it will be known that Allah Almighty stated this matter addressing those very people who were deserving of Hell. Addressing them, it was uttered that with you the very treatment has happened of which you were deserving. Allah is not one to commit wrongdoing to His servants at all. This same interpretation of Al-Kashshaf has been presented by some people of this era in this color: that Allah Almighty since He is the Creator, if He, being the Creator, wrongs His creation, it would become a very great wrongdoing, whereas this matter is contrary to the majesty of Allah Almighty that He become a very great wrongdoer, for which reason Allah Almighty negated His being 'zallam'.
Those who say this matter perhaps do not know that the great majority of our theologians (Mutakallimin) has, based on this very argument that God is the Creator, adopted this stance that the ruling of wrongdoing and injustice cannot be applied to any action of God at all. If He throws the good into Hell, this too is justice; and if He grants Paradise to the disobedient, this too is justice. Reflect, and it will be known that this statement of the old theologians, despite being incorrect, is not as incorrect as the statement of the new theologians of this era is.
Secondly, these gentlemen have also not reflected on this aspect: that Allah Almighty the negation of wrongdoing and injustice He made from His essence, He made based on His attributes of justice, mercy, and wisdom. On this foundation, its negation has nowhere been made that Allah Almighty is the Creator, for which reason He cannot become a great wrongdoer.
Another aspect of the issue also remained hidden from the eyes of these gentlemen: that if this philosophy is accepted as correct, its meaning would be that if wrongdoing occurs from a non-creator upon the creations of God, though it be a wrongdoing no matter how great, they will not be 'zallam'. To the extent that Pharaoh, Haman, Nimrod, and Shaddad too will not be great wrongdoers according to them, because they were not creators, and great wrongdoing is only the one that is issued from the creator. It is not known in which school these gentlemen read this theology: that He who is the Creator and Provider, if He wrongs, He becomes 'zallam'; and those who are neither creators nor providers, if they inflict wrongdoings, they remain merely wrongdoers (zalim).
In any case, all these hair-splittings, whether they be of the ancient theologians and commentators or of the new translators and commentators, are the result of unfamiliarity with this style of the Arabic language, of which we have carried out the explanation above.

Yawma naqulu li-jahannama hali-mtala'ti wa taqulu hal mim mazid 30
The Self-Sufficiency of Allah Almighty 
He stated: remember well that day on which We will ask Hell, 'Have you become completely filled or not!' It will reply, 'Are there any more!' This question and answer can be the characterization of the situation, and can be the statement of the event too. All things that Allah Almighty has made, they all understand His questions and also give their answers. He is Able upon this too that whenever He wills, He can make even silent things speaking. In the Quran, it is: when the limbs and organs of the criminals will give testimony against them, they will ask them in amazement, 'Why did you give testimony against us?' They will reply: 'antaqanallahalladhi antaqa kulla shay'' (Fussilat: 21) (We were made to speak by that God who made everything speak).
This question and answer is a picture of the self-sufficiency of Allah Almighty and His boundless wrath: that while filling the residents of Hell into Hell, not the slightest hesitation will overcome Him; rather, with complete self-sufficiency, He will cause all to be thrown into Hell and then will ask Hell, 'Why, have you become completely filled or not?' The meaning is that no one should remain in this misunderstanding that after throwing such countless creation into Hell, Allah Almighty will face some regret that 'I threw so much of My own created creation into the Fire'; rather, the state of the passion of His wrath will be such that if there are more, He will make them the fuel of Hell too.
'Wa taqulu hal mim mazid': This reply of Hell can have two aspects: one is that with whichever passion of wrath Allah Almighty will state this question, with that same passion it too will reply that 'if there are more, there is great capacity inside me, bring them too'; second is that seeing this abundance of the residents of Hell, its courage will give way and it will reply in a discouraged manner, 'Are there any more!' Although both meanings can emerge from the words of the verse, I give preference to the first. One cause for this is that in adopting this meaning, complete correspondence takes place between the passion of wrath of Allah Almighty upon the residents of Hell and the passion of wrath of Hell, and this very matter is suitable for Hell, that it execute the command of its Lord with full passion and zeal. Second is that in the Quran, in various places, the description of the expanse of Hell has occurred in such a manner that it appears that those people are being warned who are trapped in the misunderstanding as to from where such an expansive Hell will be brought to fill so much creation. Take a look at what we have written under the verse 'laha sab'atu abwab...' (Al-Hijr: 44). The third cause is that in some places, the picture of Hell has been drawn in such a manner that when the residents of Hell are thrown into it, it will be known that it is bursting with the passion of wrath. 'Idha ulqu fiha sami'u laha shahiqaw-wa hiya tafur, takadu tamayyazu minal-ghayz' (Al-Mulk: 7-8). This same subject is in Verse 106 of Surah Hud too.

Wa uzlifati-l-jannatu lil-muttaqina ghayra ba'id 31
Honoring of the Righteous 
After the residents of Hell, now this is describing the end of the righteous: that their honoring and respect will be in such a manner that Paradise, like an offering, will be brought close to them; to reach it, they will not have to bear any hardship of travel, whereas it will not be far. In my view, 'ghayra ba'id' (not far) is a state (haal) occurring from Paradise. By the bringing of this state, the objective is the expression of the reality: from the words of bringing close, no one should have the doubt that it will be brought close from very far, in which some interval will be spent. Rather, it will be completely close, but for the honoring of the people of Paradise, it will be brought even closer. The word 'ba'id', though masculine, its occurrence as a state from the feminine is not contrary to Arabic. Zamakhshari has declared it permissible, and I agree with his opinion.

Hadha ma tu'aduna li-kulli awwabin hafiz (32)
See Tadabbur-i-Quran, Volume 3, pages 606-607. 
Meaning: bringing Paradise close, with these words, the permit to enter it will be given to the people of Paradise: 'this is that reward with which you were promised in the world through the prophets and messengers. Now the fulfillment of this promise is taking place.' Before the imperfect verb (mudari'), according to the rule of Arabic, a defective verb is omitted, i.e., 'ma kuntum tu'adun'. By 'hadha', the pointer is toward this Paradise, but here, according to the rule of Arabic, by way of interpretation from it, the reward and blessing are meant. Examples of this style have passed behind.
'Li-kulli awwabin hafiz': This is the description of that character of the people of Paradise based on which they were declared deserving of Paradise. Above, the character of the people of Hell has been described in Verses 5-27. Take a look at it. Now, in contrast to it, the character of the deservers of Paradise is being described: that they were regularly returning toward their Lord, protectors of His limits and boundaries. Reflect on both these attributes, and it will be known that one of them relates to the heart and the second relates to action. If man's heart is alive and awake, within all the bustles of life, his heart regularly remains returning toward his Lord; at no time does such a state of heedlessness or rebellion overcome him that he has no consciousness of the limits and prohibitions of God either and he breaks them to pieces. If due to some enticement of the self, transgression is issued from him sometimes, his heart is immediately warned and through repentance and seeking forgiveness, he reforms his attitude again. Within these two words, both the inward and outward of a true believer have been described. From this, this reality also became clear: to be a faithful believer, abandonment of the world is not necessary; rather, his real test is that while remaining in this battlefield of life, he proves his being 'awwab' (oft-returning) and 'hafiz' (guardian of limits). Remember this matter too: in Islam, for every person, presenting oneself five times in the day and night at the mosque is for keeping this very 'awwabiyyah' alive. In this manner, the believer remains connected to his center of gravity, and the devil never gains so much control over him to trap him in indecencies and evils. 'Inna-s-salata tanha 'ani-l-fahsha'i wal-munkar' (Al-Ankabut: 45).

Man khashiya-r-rahmana bil-ghaybi wa ja'a bi-qalbim-munib (33)
Above, regarding the deservers of Hell, this matter was stated: they remained doubtful in the chapter of the Resurrection and in contrast to Allah, they made other supports. There we explained that both these things hold, in reality, the status of the mother of diseases. Here, in contrast to it, regarding the people of Paradise, He stated: these did not become trapped in any illusion of this sort, but remained fearing the Beneficent God (Allah) while in the Unseen, and since they did not search for any support other than God, for this reason, they presented themselves in the presence of their Lord with such a heart whose entire attention remained toward their Lord alone, because all their hopes were attached to Him, they did not attach expectations to anyone else.
A Great Wisdom of Religion 
In 'man khashiya-r-rahman', the reference to the attribute of 'Rahman' (the Beneficent) of God is to draw attention toward a great wisdom of religion. That is that this is the inevitable demand of the attribute of mercy of Allah Almighty, that He must ultimately bring such a day too in which He rewards those who spent a life of 'awwab' and 'hafiz' in this world, and punishes those who played tricks in it becoming 'kaffar' and 'anid'. If He were not to do so, its meaning is that—Allah forbid—goodness and evil, wrongdoing and justice both are equal in His eyes. Whereas this matter is completely contrary to His being Rahman and Rahim.線 Consequently, in another place, Allah Almighty has described this inevitable demand of His mercy: that He will surely bring the Day of Resurrection. 'Kataba 'ala nafsihi-r-rahmah, la-yajma'annakum ila yawmi-l-qiyamah' (Al-An'am: 12) (He has prescribed mercy upon Himself. He will surely gather you until the Day of Resurrection). The people of faith, since they understood well this attribute of their Lord and its demands, for this reason, they considered the Resurrection an unalterable reality and remained regularly awake for it.
In 'bil-ghayb', 'bi' is for causality/state. Regarding the deniers of the Resurrection, it was stated above that on the Day of Resurrection when realities are revealed before them, it will be said to them: 'Laqad kunta fi ghaflatim-min hadha fa-kashafna 'anka ghita'aka fa-basaruka-l-yawma hadid' (Qaf: 22) (You remained in heedlessness of this day, so We removed your veil from before your eyes, so today your sight is very sharp). In contrast to it, the state of these people of faith remained such: remaining in this very world, and without witnessing the realities of the Hereafter with the eyes, merely on the basis of the arguments of the horizons and souls and the teaching of the prophets, they believed in the Hereafter and remained fearing their Lord.
By 'qalbi-m-munib' is meant that heart which in every state of grief, comfort, and hope remained attentive toward its Lord alone. In no state, turning away its face from its Lord, did it attach expectations to anyone else.

Udkhuluha bi-salamin dhalika yawmu-l-khulud (34)
Eternal Kingdom 
Meaning: it will be said to them, enter this Paradise with peace. Now in it, neither will there be any regret of the past for you nor any fear of the future. The time of the granting of the eternal kingdom with which you were promised has arrived. This kingdom will never be seized from you, nor will any breach ever be created in it. In Verse 46 of Surah Al-Hijr, this same matter was stated as follows: 'Udkhuluha bi-salamin aminin' (Enter it with peace, secure, being carefree of every fear). In the Quran, the mention of this matter has also appeared in various places: that the angels will welcome the people of Paradise with salam; rather, the mention of the salam and message of Allah Almighty has also appeared, but their place is other. Here, the objective is the very one which is gaining support from the verse of Surah Al-Hijr.

Lahum ma yasha'una fiha wa ladayna mazid 35
In it, they will get all that they will desire, and with Us there will be more too. The meaning is that what they will desire, that they will get necessarily, but with Us there will be such blessings too of which they will have no imagination at all to be able to wish for them, but We, without their desiring, will grant those blessings to them too. Toward this very reality, in other words, it has been pointed as follows: 'Fa-la ta'lamu nafsum-ma ukhfiya lahum min qurrati a'yun' (As-Sajdah: 17) (So no soul knows what has been hidden for them of comfort for the eyes).

-4 The Subject of the Upcoming Verses 36-45 
Ahead are the concluding verses of the Surah. First, the Quraysh have been warned: do not dare to fight God in the pride of your current power and might. How many nations have passed before you who were far greater than you in power and might, but when God seized them, they could not save themselves. If you possess hearts that understand and ears that listen, there is a great lesson of warning for you in their stories.
After that, comfort has been given to the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him): be patient upon whatever these opponents say. The Resurrection is bound to happen. We did not become tired by creating the heavens and the earth to be unable to raise the people alive again after death. So keep your ears plugged in. That day is coming when the caller of God will call out and all will stand up coming out from the graves. So be patient and maintain the arrangement of prayer. Your responsibility is not to make the people believers. Rather, it is merely to warn through this Quran those people of Our threat who want to be warned... Please recite the verses.
5. Word Research and Explanation of Verses 
Wa kam ahlakna qablahum min qarnin hum ashaddu minhum batshan fa-naqqabu fi-l-bilad hal mim mahis 36
Warning to the Quraysh 
This is a warning to the Quraysh: that they should not remain in this arrogance that they possess great power and grandeur, and cannot be moved from their place. How many nations have passed before them who were greater than them in power and grandeur, but Allah Almighty destroyed them. Among them, if some escaped from being destroyed, they stood moving into different countries, wherever one's horn found space, in search of some place of escape.
The meaning of 'naqqabu fi-l-bilad' is: 'saru fiha talabal-lil-mahrab' (and he stood moving in the earth in search of a place of escape). 'Hal mim mahis' (Is there any place of escape?) is the description of the motive of their moving: that in search of a place to hide the head, wherever one's face rose, he stood moving toward it.
When destruction comes upon a nation, its state is exactly this. The controlling elements of the nation, who are responsible for all the corruption, are destroyed; those who remain among the common public, after the breaking to pieces of the national assembly, becoming scattered, turn toward whichever direction they expect to find refuge in.
Different Forms of the Destruction of Nations 
Among the nations whose states have been described in the Quran, some, it is known, were completely destroyed by the divine punishment, for example, the people of Noah, 'Ad, and Thamud, etc. The state of some nations emerged such that their luxury-indulgers and arrogant ones were destroyed, and those who were their followers and common public became scattered here and there. The form of the destruction of Pharaoh and his nation emerged exactly like this. He himself, along with his dignitaries and entire army, was drowned in the sea. Those who remained behind became scattered in search of refuge out of fear of the destruction of the government and of their rivals.
States close to this appeared in the kingdom of Saba. Countless individuals became the offering of that very flood which came upon them. Those who remained were compelled, due to the region becoming barren and the economic state becoming completely wretched, to turn toward other regions in search of refuge. The destructions that came upon the Jews, their nature too was this very one. Those who escaped from killing and plunder wandered becoming vagabonds in every corner of the world. Even in our own history, highly warning examples of this are present. The destructions that came upon Baghdad, upon Cordoba, upon Delhi, are their states not mentioned in history? Read them, and it will be known that in this verse, it is not a warning to the Quraysh alone, but for ourselves too there is a great lesson in it, provided we acquire a lesson from it!
Removal of a Misunderstanding 
Some people have taken the meaning of 'naqqabu fi-l-bilad' to be: that they, in their period of ascent, searched out every corner of the world through their victorious conquests. But this matter is not correct. The word 'tanqib', in Arabic, does not come for victorious roamings, but comes for boring into the earth out of fear and dread, in search of a place of refuge. The experts of lexicography have described this very meaning of it. Above we have given its reference. Support is gained for this from the evidences of the speech of the Arabs too, and the subsequent piece 'hal mim mahis' is in a highly clear manner in favor of this meaning. Otherwise, this sentence will remain completely out of place.

Inna fi dhalika la-dhikra li-man kana lahu qalbun aw alqa-s-sam'a wa huwa shahid 37
Warning to the Quraysh and Comfort to the Holy Prophet 
The pointer of 'dhalika' is toward the story of those very nations reference to whom has occurred in a summary manner in the verse above. It is stated that in the story of these nations, sufficient material of warning is present, provided there are hearts that acquire warning and ears that listen to the matter with attention.
The word 'qalb' (heart) is in its true meaning, i.e., the living heart. Allah Almighty made the heart to sense, to acquire warning, and to think and understand. As long as man's heart performs this task, until that time his heart is alive, and as long as the heart is alive, until that time the man too is alive. The cause of this is that the true life of man is from the life of his heart alone. If the heart lost these specific characteristics, then the man too is dead, even if how much blood goes around running in his veins.
'Aw alqa-s-sam'a wa huwa shahid', meaning: if the heart is not completely awake, at least this much matter should be inside man that if a reasonable man recites a matter to him, he should listen to it with attention. This attention too is a cause of great good and blessing for man. From this also, many a time, the heedlessness of the heart is removed and its ability to accept warning is revived. But the person who is so unfortunate that neither is his heart awake nor is he ready to open his ears to listen to the matter of any reasonable man, from where will any reasonable matter find a way inside such a man?
Although the context of the discourse here is of warning and reproach to the Quraysh, within it there is comfort and solace for the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) too: that the Quran you are reciting, if it is not affecting the hearts of the leaders of the Quraysh, its original cause is that neither is the ability to accept effect present inside their hearts, nor are they ready to listen to your matter with attention by opening their ears. So after all, how can you descend your matter into their hearts?

Wa laqad khalaqna-s-samawati wal-arda wa ma baynahuma fi sittati ayyamiw-wa ma massana mil-lughub. Fasbir 'ala ma yaquluna wa sabbih bi-hamdi rabbika qabla tulu'i-sh-shamsi wa qabla-l-ghurub. Wa mina-l-layli fa-sabbihhu wa adbara-s-sujud (38-40)
Exhortation of Patience to the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) 
Now the direction of the discourse has clearly turned toward the subject of solace and comfort and the exhortation of patience for the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him). Its introduction has been raised in this manner: We did not become tired in the creation of heaven and earth to remain incapable of creating them a second time. Rest satisfied that the day with which they are being threatened will come. Be patient upon their words, and to attain this patience, keep the maximum arrangement of prayer.
The Meaning of 'fi sittati ayyam' 
'Wa laqad khalaqna-s-samawati wal-arda wa ma baynahuma fi sittati ayyam': The mention of the heavens and the earth being created in six days has passed in the preceding Surahs too. There we clarified that by 'ayyam' (days) here are meant divine days, for which reason these are in the meaning of periods (epochs). This matter also we clarified: that when the mention of the creation of the heavens and the earth occurs with the restriction of six days, the objective from it is to draw attention toward that arrangement which is observed in the creation of this universe, and which is proof of the fact that this world has neither appeared as a chance accident, nor is it the play and amusement of a player, but it is a purposeful factory, and the inevitable requirement of its being purposeful is that after it should come such a day in which its objective becomes clear.
'Wa ma massana mil-lughub', meaning: no one should become trapped in this misunderstanding that due to working continuously for six days, tiredness overcame Us and now we will not be able to have the courage to create this world a second time. We, in whichever manner we were fresh before, in that same manner are fresh now too, and in whichever manner we did not remain incapable of creating the first time, in that same manner we will not remain incapable of creating a second time. The style of this phrase is somewhat sarcastic.
