The meaning is that those who are considering the creation of this world anew as far-fetched from possibility, let them rest satisfied that our strength and breath is established in whichever manner it was before. Not a hair's difference has come in it. Within it, by implication, there is also an exposure of that idea of the Jews which is in the Torah, that Allah Almighty created the heavens and the earth in six days and rested on the seventh day.

'Fasbir 'ala ma yaqulun': This is the exhortation of patience to the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him), that be patient upon whatever they are doing in your denial. By 'ma yaqulun', the pointer is toward that type of words of the deniers an example of which passed in Verses 2-3: 'Fa-qalal-kafiruna hadha shay'un 'ajib. A-idha mitna wa kunna turaba dhalika raj'um ba'id' (So the disbelievers said, 'This is a highly strange thing! When we die and become dust, will we be raised anew? This returning is highly far-fetched!!'). The meaning is that this world which Allah created with this arrangement will inevitably reach its objective. We did not become tired by creating it to be unable to create it a second time, but those who are blind of heart will accept this reality only at the time when they witness everything with the eyes. Upon the opposition of people of this sort you have to be patient in any case, so be patient until the reality manifests before them in such a manner that no room at all for denial remains.
The Strategy to Attain Patience 
'Wa sabbih bi-hamdi rabbika qabla tulu'i-sh-shamsi wa qabla-l-ghurub': This told the strategy to attain patience: that for it, keep the maximum arrangement of prayer. Without a deep connection with Allah Almighty, that patience cannot be produced which, despite the opposition of the opponents, can keep a man's steps firmly established on the path of truth. Toward this very reality, it has been pointed in another place as follows: 'wa ma sabruka illa billah' (Al-Nahl: 127) (And your patience is not except through the assistance of Allah). The single medium of seeking assistance from Allah Almighty is prayer; for this reason, wherever the instruction of patience has been given to the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) against the opponents, the emphasis on the arrangement of prayer has surely been given there. Examples of this have passed behind in various places too, and in the upcoming Surahs also highly clear and effective examples will come.
'Sabbih bi-hamdi rabbika': This characterization of prayer is from the aspect of the remembrance of Allah Almighty. This remembrance is compounded of two elements: one 'tasbih' (glorification), second 'hamd' (praise). In 'tasbih', the aspect of purification (tanzih) is dominant, i.e., declaring Allah Almighty pure and free from those matters that are contrary to His majesty. In 'hamd', the aspect of affirmation (ithbat) is manifest, i.e., declaring Him characterized by those attributes that are befitting of His majesty. This negation and this affirmation both joining firmly establish the correct concept of Allah Almighty in the heart, and from this deep establishment alone the correct relationship of the servant is established with Allah Almighty, which is the foundation of all patience and trust. If any weakness or lack of balance is produced in them from any aspect, the concept of man regarding Allah Almighty becomes incorrect, and this mistake completely turns upside down his entire system of thought and action.
Times of Prayer and Their Wisdom 
'Qabla tulu'i-sh-shamsi wa qabla-l-ghurub, wa mina-l-layli fa-sabbihhu wa adbara-s-sujud': This pointed toward those specific times in which the praise and glorification of Allah Almighty is required with special arrangement. Though the remembrance of Allah is life-giving at all times—from His remembrance alone the heart remains alive, fresh, and satisfied—just as in the other works of this world there is consideration of times, seasons, and weather, similarly in the acts of worship of Allah Almighty also there is regard for times and hours. By reflecting upon the times that have been made specific for prayers, it becomes known that within the twenty-four hours of day and night, those times which are a sign of some major change in this universe, which are a reminder of the grandeur and power of the true Disposer of the world, and in which the other prominent things of this universe also bow down before their Creator, those very times have been fixed for our prayers. Among them, the most prominent and highly exclusive time is the time of Fajr, when the night rolls up its carpet, the stars bow down in prostration before their Lord, and the dawn of the new day rises. And then exactly opposite to it is the time of 'Asr, when the activities of the day enter their final stage and the sun lowers its head to bow down in prostration before its Lord. Consequently, in the verse, first it pointed toward both these times. By 'qabla tulu'i-sh-shams' (before the rising of the sun), the time of Fajr is meant, and through 'qabla-l-ghurub' (before the setting), the pointer is toward 'Asr. The importance that both these prayers possess in our religion has been clarified in both the Quran and the Hadith.

The Night Prayers 
'Wa mina-l-layli fa-sabbihhu': After mentioning the two important prayers of the day, He stated that similarly, in a part of the night, Tahajjud. Tahajjud, though it is not included in the obligations (fara'id), from the aspect of the training of patience, the maximum importance is possessed by this very prayer. Its explanation has passed in various places, and in the commentary of Surah Al-Muzzammil, inshallah, its further explanation will come.
The Correct Meaning of 'adbar as-sujud' 
'Wa adbara-s-sujud': 'Adbar' is the plural of 'dubur', whose meaning is behind/after. Generally, people have described its meaning to be: perform His glorification after the prostrations (prayers) too. But my mind goes toward this: that here 'sujud' is in the infinitive meaning and from it is meant the prostration of the sun, the prayers of before whose rising and setting have already been mentioned in the piece above. Meaning: just as before the rising and setting of the sun there are times of prayer, similarly after the prostration of the sun too there are times of glorification. Since the mention of the sun had occurred before, for this reason, there was no need to specify it a second time. The context of the word 'adbar' was sufficient to point toward it, that here the mention is of the prayer after its prostration, the prayers of before whose rising and setting were mentioned first. If between both pieces the words 'wa mina-l-layli fa-sabbihhu' had not come, no trouble would have faced the understanding of the matter. In that case, the structure of the discourse would be like this: perform glorification in the times before the rising of the sun and its setting and after its prostration. In this structure, the mind of every person would easily move toward this, that by 'sujud' is meant 'sujud-i-shams' (prostration of the sun). But if the structure of the discourse were like this, an important point of the wisdom of religion would not become clear which has been clarified in this verse: that in it, the order of prayers has been described according to their importance and grandeur in the religion. This order required that first of all the mention of Fajr should come, after that of 'Asr. Consequently, with the words 'qabla tulu'i-sh-shamsi wa qabla-l-ghurub', the mention of both occurred, and we have mentioned that in both the Quran and the Hadith, special emphasis has been given to the importance of these prayers. After that, through 'wa mina-l-layli fa-sabbihhu', the mention of 'Isha and Tahajjud prayers was made, which possess that same importance in the prayers of the night which the Fajr and 'Asr prayers possess in the prayers of the day. After that, through the words 'adbara-s-sujud', the pointer is toward Zuhr and Maghrib prayers, which relate to the times of the declining of the sun (duluk-i-shams) and the prostration of the sun.
Under Verse 78 of Surah Bani Isra'il, 'aqimi-s-salata li-duluki-sh-shams', we have explained the different stages of the declining of the sun. Here the word is 'sujud', and it too has different degrees. When this word is used for the sun and other planets, from it are meant all the stages of their decline and setting. Here too, all these very stages can be: meaning, when it declines from the meridian, when it comes below the horizon of vision, then when it disappears from the horizon. The Zuhr, 'Asr, and Maghrib prayers are in these three times. Here, the mention of 'Asr prayer, under that wisdom toward which we pointed, has occurred with special arrangement through the words 'qabla-l-ghurub' above, for which reason two prayers remained. One Zuhr, which is after the first stage of the prostration of the sun, and the second Maghrib, which is after the final stage of its prostration. It is as if in this verse, the pointer is toward all those times in which our five prayers are recited. The Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him), through his teaching, made the codification of these very times.
This subject has been described in the Quran in various styles in different places, and we have been explaining it everywhere; here copying the parallels will cause length. For the satisfaction of the readers, we copy only one verse of Surah Taha, in which this entire subject has come in highly clear words.
It is stated:
'Fasbir 'ala ma yaquluna wa sabbih bi-hamdi rabbika qabla tulu'i-sh-shamsi wa qabla ghurubiha wa min ana'i-l-layli fa-sabbih wa atrafa-n-nahar' (130)
So be patient over what they say and exalt [Allah] with praise of your Lord before the rising of the sun and before its setting; and during periods of the night exalt [Him] and at the ends of the day.
The explanation of the implications of the words 'atraf an-nahar' has already passed in the commentary of Surah Taha. If details are required, please take a look at it.
The explanation of the implications of the words 'atraf an-nahar' has already passed in the commentary of Surah Taha; if details are required, please take a look at it.

Wastami' yawma yunadi-l-munadi mim-makanin qarib 41
Keep Your Ears Plugged In at Every Moment for the Cry of the Caller of the Resurrection 
The meaning of 'istima'' is to keep the ears plugged in with attention. He stated: these people deny this day,
See Tadabbur-i-Quran, Volume 3, pages 773-774. 
See Tadabbur-i-Quran, Volume 4, pages 244-245. 
so let them deny, you do not care for their words. Rather, to listen to the voice of that caller, keep the ears plugged in regularly, who will call out from a highly close place. By this caller is meant that very caller of the blowing of the Trumpet whose mention has passed above with the words 'wa nufikha fi-s-suri dhalika yawmul-wa'id'. Through 'mim-makanin qarib' (from a close place), the pointer is toward this reality: today to these heedless ones these matters appear of a very far time and highly far-fetched from imagination, but on that day every person will feel that it is being called out as if in his very ears. This style of speech is exactly of that type like 'fartaqib yawma ta'ti-s-sama'' (10) is in Surah Ad-Dukhan. From it, the objective is, for one, the expression of the certainty of the event and its dreadfulness. Second, from it, the pointer is also toward this matter: consider the time of its manifestation to have arrived, it is not known when the caller may call out.
In the verse, the address is to the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him), and this address is highly eloquent. From it, this matter emerges: if these dull people are carefree of this great hour, leave them to their state, you remain regularly in the expectation of its manifestation at every moment. In the matter of the Resurrection, in whichever manner a wise man should remain alert, this verse is its correct picture. Above in Verse 19, this subject has passed that every person should consider it standing over his head. This is attached along with the death of every person, and death, it is not known at which hour it may arrive; foolish are those people who consider it a very far time after it.

Yawma yasma'una-s-sayhata bil-haqq dhalika yawmu-l-khuruj 42
This 'yawm' (day) is an appositive (badal) from the 'yawm' above, and by 'sayhah' is meant the cry of the blowing of the Trumpet. By 'haqq' is meant—as the explanation passed in Verse 19, the Resurrection. Characterizing it by this word manifests this reality that it is an unalterable reality and bound to happen.
He stated: keep the ears plugged in regularly for the voice of the Trumpet of that day, on which day these deniers will listen to its cry in the capacity of the announcement of the manifestation of this bound-to-happen event. That day will be of their coming out from the graves.

Inna nahnu nuhyi wa numitu wa ilayna-l-masir 43
This stated the argument for that coming out: We alone give life and We alone cause death, and this is such a reality that no one can deny it, so why will it become difficult for Us to create them alive a second time? This argument has passed above with explanation.
'Wa ilayna-l-masir': This placed another clear result of that upper verse forward: when We alone became the givers of life and We alone the causes of death, from this not only did this matter become necessary that We are Able upon creating alive a second time too, but this matter also became necessary that the return of everyone will be toward Us alone. If someone is trapped in this misunderstanding that if the Resurrection happened, the alleged partners and intercessors of the leader will become ones to come to his use, then let him remove this misunderstanding from his mind; those who have no part in the matter of life, who possess no choice in the matter of death, how will they become a place of refuge and shelter in the Hereafter!

Yawma tashaqqaqu-l-ardu 'anhum sira'a dhalika hashrun 'alayna yasir 44
Picture of Coming Out from the Graves on the Day of Resurrection 
This is the picture of people coming out from the graves on that day. He stated: after the blowing of the Trumpet, the earth will split from over them and they will be coming out from it highly rapidly. 'Sira'an' (rapidly) occurs as a state (haal) from the genitive pronoun. The picture of this coming out rapidly has been drawn in other places of the Quran in such a manner that in whichever manner moths and locusts come out, in that same manner people will come out from the graves.
'Dhalika hashrun 'alayna yasir', meaning: no one should remain in this misunderstanding that to extract the people from the earth, some major arrangement will have to be made which We will not be able to do, or a major time will be spent in it. This entire task will happen in the blink of an eye, like a snap of fingers. This is highly easy for Us.

Nahnu a'lamu bima yaquluna wa ma anta 'alayhim bi-jabbar fa-dhakkir bil-qur'ani may-yakhafu wa'id 45
Above in Verse 39, He stated: 'fasbir 'ala ma yaqulun'. So whatever they say, We know it well. The meaning is that when We know it well, why do you grieve. Leave the matter upon Us. We will cure it. You go on performing your work with patience.
'Wa ma anta 'alayhim bi-jabbar': And remember this matter too, that you have not been sent being appointed upon the task of making them believers, that if they did not believe, its questioning should be from you. Your work is only to remind the people, that you keep doing regularly. If they did not believe, its end they themselves will suffer.
On Whichever Subject the Surah Began, Upon That It Concluded 
'Fa-dhakkir bil-qur'ani may-yakhafu wa'id': Upon this very matter He concluded the Surah with which He had begun it. From the mention of the Quran alone it had begun, of its reminder alone He informed in the end: your responsibility is only reminding (tadhkir), and for reminding, this Quran is sufficient, so through its medium remind those people who want to fear My threat. Those who do not want to fear and for your verification are demanding some punishment from you, leave them to their destiny. They will see its end.
By the grace of Allah Almighty, the commentary on this Surah concluded on these lines. And our final call is that all praise is due to Allah, Lord of the worlds.
